subUnits.title: 11.1 Solving Basic Economic Problems: Resource Allocation and Efficiency
notes: • Different economic systems provide different answers
• Decision: Which goods and services to produce, in what quantities
- In market economies, consumer demand guides production
- Example: High demand for smartphones drives production in Nigeria
- Production limited by available land, labour, capital, entrepreneurship
- Countries specialize in goods they can produce efficiently
- Example: Nigeria producing oil, cocoa, cassava based on resource endowments
- In command/mixed economies, government sets production priorities
- Strategic sectors may be prioritized for development
- Example: Nigerian government prioritizing agriculture and manufacturing
- Available technology determines feasible production options
- Example: Mobile money technology enabling financial inclusion
ASCII Diagram: Factors Influencing 'What to Produce'
• Decision: Which production techniques and factor combinations to use
- Relative costs of labour, capital, land influence technique choice
- Example: Nigerian farms using more labour due to low wages
- Available technology and worker skills determine feasible methods
- Training and technology transfer expand options
- Example: Adoption of improved seeds in Nigerian agriculture
- Small-scale vs large-scale methods have different requirements
- Economies of scale may favour larger operations
- Example: Large bakeries vs small local bakeries in Nigeria
- Production methods may be chosen for environmental sustainability
- Example: Regulations on industrial emissions in Nigeria
ASCII Diagram: Factors Influencing 'How to Produce'
• Decision: How output is distributed among members of society
- In market economies, income determines purchasing power
- Those with higher incomes consume more goods
- Example: Income inequality affecting access to goods in Nigeria
- Taxes and transfers can alter market-based distribution
- Example: Conditional cash transfers to poor households in Nigeria
- Community support systems supplement market distribution
- Example: Extended family support in Nigerian society
- Geographic and institutional barriers affect access
- Infrastructure and institutions enable market participation
- Example: Rural-urban divide affecting access to goods in Nigeria
ASCII Diagram: Factors Influencing 'For Whom to Produce'
• Definition: Using resources to maximize societal welfare
- Using best available technology and practices
- Example: Nigerian firms adopting lean manufacturing
- Example: Investment in sectors with highest social returns
- Adapting to changing conditions and knowledge
- Example: Nigerian fintech adapting to digital economy needs
• Poor power supply increases production costs
• Bad roads raise transport costs and spoilage
• Example: Manufacturers spending 40% of costs on power
• Corruption diverts resources from productive uses
• Example: Land disputes delaying development projects
• Skills mismatch between education and labour market
• Shortage of technical and vocational skills
• Example: Graduate unemployment alongside skills shortages
• Multiple taxes and levies increase business costs
• Example: Frequent changes in import policies affecting planning
• Invest in power, transport, digital infrastructure
• Example: Power sector reforms to improve electricity supply
• Strengthen property rights and contract enforcement
• Reduce corruption through transparency and accountability
• Streamline regulations to reduce business costs
• Example: Ease of Doing Business reforms in Nigeria
• Align education with labour market needs
• Example: N-Power and other youth skills programmes
• Coordinate policies across sectors and levels of government
• Engage stakeholders in policy design and implementation
• Example: National Development Plan with multi-stakeholder input
• Example: Support for agritech and fintech innovation
• What: Prioritize food crops for food security, cash crops for export
• How: Mix of traditional and modern techniques, appropriate mechanization
• For whom: Ensure smallholder farmers benefit from value chains
• Efficiency: Improve extension services, input access, market linkages
• What: Focus on import substitution and export-oriented industries
• How: Adopt appropriate technology, improve productivity
• For whom: Create jobs, develop local content, build skills
• Efficiency: Address power, infrastructure, and skills constraints
• What: Expand digital services, tourism, creative industries
• How: Leverage digital platforms, invest in human capital
• For whom: Ensure inclusive access, especially for youth and women
• Efficiency: Improve regulation, competition, and infrastructure
• 'Efficiency means only profit': Efficiency includes social welfare, not just private profit
• 'Government always improves efficiency': Government intervention can also create inefficiencies
• 'Market solutions always best': Markets can fail; appropriate intervention may improve outcomes
• 'Efficiency conflicts with equity': Well-designed policies can promote both efficiency and equity
• Solving basic economic problems requires balancing multiple objectives
• Different economic systems offer different approaches to resource allocation
• Efficiency is crucial for development but must be balanced with equity
• Nigeria's mixed economy requires coordinated public and private action
• Improving resource use efficiency is key to sustainable development
• Understanding these concepts helps in policy analysis and business strategy
diagramDescriptions: Diagram showing the three basic economic questions and their interrelationships
Flowchart: Factors influencing production decisions (what, how, for whom)
Graph showing types of efficiency: productive, allocative, dynamic
Nigerian context: Barriers to efficiency and strategies for improvement
Policy framework: Coordinated approach to resource allocation in mixed economy
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Basic economic problems resource allocation efficiency SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: The question 'What to produce?' is primarily influenced by:
opts: Consumer preferences, resources, technology, and policy
quiz.explanation: Production decisions are influenced by multiple factors including consumer demand, resource availability, technology, and government priorities.

quiz.q: In a market economy, 'How to produce?' is mainly determined by:
quiz.explanation: In market economies, firms choose production techniques based on relative factor costs and available technology to minimize costs.

quiz.q: The question 'For whom to produce?' relates to:
opts: Distribution of output among society members
quiz.explanation: This question addresses how the output of production is distributed among different groups in society.

opts: Production occurs at the lowest possible cost
quiz.explanation: Productive efficiency means producing goods at minimum cost using the best available technology and practices.

opts: Resources are allocated to produce goods consumers value most
quiz.explanation: Allocative efficiency means resources are used to produce the combination of goods that maximizes societal welfare.

quiz.q: A barrier to resource efficiency in Nigeria is:
opts: Poor power supply increasing production costs
quiz.explanation: Inadequate power supply forces businesses to use expensive generators, raising costs and reducing productive efficiency.

quiz.q: Improving human capital in Nigeria would help efficiency by:
opts: Better matching skills to labour market needs
quiz.explanation: Better alignment of education and training with labour market needs improves productivity and resource allocation.

quiz.q: In Nigeria's mixed economy, resource allocation is determined by:
opts: Combination of market mechanisms and government intervention
quiz.explanation: Nigeria's mixed economy uses both market signals and government policy to allocate resources and address market failures.

quiz.q: Dynamic efficiency is important because it:
opts: Enables innovation and adaptation over time
quiz.explanation: Dynamic efficiency allows economies to innovate, adopt new technologies, and adapt to changing conditions for long-term growth.

quiz.q: A strategy for improving resource use in Nigeria is:
opts: Infrastructure investment, institutional reforms, and human capital development
quiz.explanation: Sustainable efficiency improvements require multi-faceted approach addressing infrastructure, institutions, and human capital.
